item.title: Off target
item.description: Michael Ramirez cartoon on Iran war, NATO, President Donald Trump.

item.title: ‘Back to the Stone Ages’
item.description: Donald Trump tries to make his case for war with Iran.

item.title: The lesson of Pam Bondi’s firing
item.description: The AG showed no independence from the White House. That didn’t save her job.

item.title: Trump: Send Iran to the Stone Age
item.description:
An upcoming Washington Post Opinions show. Stay tuned.
00:00 - Monologue 03:13 - Trump: Send Iran to the Stone Age 16:43 - Can Trump make Iran war worth it for U.S.? 29:38 - Hegseth exonerates Army helicopter crew over Kid Rock flyby

item.title: My husband’s deployment taught me the true cost of war
item.description: The invisible toll of loving someone deployed, the Jones Act, NATO and Iran, in readers’ eyes.

item.title: ‘Should I postpone my trip?’ Your questions about outbreaks, answered.
item.description: Who should get additional vaccines during outbreaks of infectious diseases

item.title: Investing has changed. Retirement plan regulations haven’t caught up.
item.description: Updating rules for 401(k) plans investing in alternative assets is overdue.

item.title: Challenging birthright citizenship
item.description: Edith Pritchett cartoon on President Donald Trump and Supreme Court case on birthright citizenship.

item.title: Trump’s hall-of-mirrors speech
item.description: The president perpetuated the dissonance that has characterized the Iran war from the start.

item.title: Removing a tyrant is easy. Changing a regime is hard.
item.description: It’s no surprise Iran didn’t collapse. Security states are meant to last beyond any one leader.

item.title: Trump doesn’t need an Iran deal
item.description: Instead of waiting for Tehran to agree, Trump can declare victory and impose his will in five steps.

item.title: My team is back in the Final Four. So is my whole family.
item.description: Three couches, three Illini Final Fours, one family.

item.title: Have you heard the Hum? You might not even know it.
item.description: This mysterious sound turns Times Square into a work of art.

item.title: Unions are on a comeback. Americans are paying the price.
item.description: Unions may be on the rise, but the economic reality of organized labor remains the same.

item.title: Another blow to blue-state business hubs
item.description: Apollo is eyeing a new headquarters in Florida or Texas.

item.title: Can Europe get serious on energy?
item.description: We know the Europeans can run a committee. But can they stand on their own two feet?

item.title: The Supreme Court’s middle way on birthright citizenship
item.description: The court doesn’t need to rule on the 14th Amendment, Justices Gorsuch and Kavanaugh observe.

item.title: America launches its bid to reach Mars
item.description: The Artemis II mission is an extraordinary feat of American ingenuity.

item.title: Get your own oil? Trump flip-flops on Strait of Hormuz
item.description:
An upcoming Washington Post Opinions show. Stay tuned.
00:00 - Monologue 04:23 - Get Your Own Oil? Trump Flip-Flops On Strait Of Hormuz 15:55 - The Politics Of Conversion Therapy 25:36 - Trump Wants Birthright Citizenship Revoked

item.title: Trump’s hypocrisy on voting is hard to stomach as a military man
item.description: A gap grows between leaders who make policy and those who serve and care about democracy.

item.title: Where Medicare Advantage is falling short
item.description: Seniors’ health care coverage, AI adoption and MFN drug pricing, in readers’ eyes.

item.title: Trump’s gain with Hispanics is evaporating. Republicans should worry.
item.description: The GOP’s biggest 2024 asset is turning into a midterm liability.

item.title: Cartoon by Lisa Benson
item.description: Lisa Benson cartoon on the Strait of Hormuz, Iran and the global economy.

item.title: I have to read poetry for work. You get to read it for pleasure.
item.description: The best part is what you read between the lines.

item.title: Don’t believe the claim about war and the economy
item.description: The “wartime economy” fairy tale misunderstands what GDP measures.

item.title: Braiding hair? That’ll require 500 training hours and a permit.
item.description: Louisiana’s licensing rules are strangling businesses for no good reason.

item.title: Coffin nails and the habit that defies burial
item.description: There now are so few norms to transgress, for some aspiring renegades smoking must suffice.

item.title: A D.C. transportation success story is about to be bulldozed — literally
item.description: The Trump administration wants to remove bike lanes that cut injuries by 91 percent.

item.title: Where AI will be in a year — and in a decade
item.description:
From AI-generated scams that cost Americans hundreds of dollars to voice cloning schemes, the line between real and fake is becoming harder to detect. At the same time, technology companies are setting their own limits on how these artificial intelligence tools can be used. This is raising new questions about who really controls this technology — and what that will mean for our future.
Host Megan McArdle is joined by Dean Ball, a senior fellow at the Foundation for American Innovation and former White House AI policy advisor, to unpack the rapidly shifting landscape of AI governance, what it will take to rein it in — and what comes next.
